headline: Boston College's Leavitt proves too sharp; Tennessee's ground game runs out of time
body: Sam Leavitt carved up Tennessee's secondary with surgical precision, completing 38 of 57 passes for 346 yards and three touchdowns to lead Boston College to a 34-21 victory on the road. The Eagles' passing attack found consistent rhythm throughout the afternoon, with Leavitt spreading the ball across multiple receivers and managing the game without a single turnover—a discipline that proved decisive against a Tennessee defense that couldn't generate consistent pressure.

Tennessee's rushing attack, led by Bishop's 89 yards and two touchdown runs, showed genuine teeth on the ground. The Volunteers moved the ball in chunks during stretches and kept pace early, but the passing game became a fatal constraint. MacIntyre completed just 11 of 23 attempts for 203 yards, forcing Tennessee to lean heavily on Bishop rather than opening up the play-action concepts that might have loosened Boston College's secondary. A critical fumble late in the second half handed Boston College the ball in favorable field position and momentum it never relinquished.

Boston College's victory was built on efficiency and balance. While the Eagles managed only 81 rushing yards, they didn't need more; Leavitt's poise allowed Harris to accumulate eight receptions and 81 yards, and the offense converted third downs at a rate that kept Tennessee's defense constantly fatigued and on the field. Tennessee's defense, led by Anderson's six tackles, competed hard but lacked the help needed when the offense couldn't sustain drives or protect the quarterback. By the final quarter, Boston College's cushion had grown too wide for Tennessee to close.
turningPoint: Tennessee's fumble in the third quarter shifted field position decisively in Boston College's favor and coincided with the Volunteers' offense stalling out completely, unable to answer with any scoring response.

unsungHero.name: Jefferson
unsungHero.why: Jefferson's 120 receiving yards on just three receptions showcased explosive playmaking that Tennessee desperately needed, yet the offense couldn't build sustained drives around his talent because MacIntyre's accuracy issues and the reliance on the run game left him isolated.
